title: Hilda Kibet -Hiring Manager Screen - Panzer | Managing Editor
meeting_title: Hilda Kibet -Hiring Manager Screen - Panzer | Managing Editor

transcript.text: Hello.
Hello.
How are you?
I'm fine.
Thank you.
It's a pleasure to meet you.
Good.
It's a pleasure to meet you as well.
My name is Matthew.
I'm the Chief Content Officer here at GrowthX.
That is a largely internal facing role here.
I work a lot with the managing editors and editorial directors and other folks here on client deliverable quality.

Make sure that we're delivering high-quality work and then also to articulate that and improve the pipelines and workflows to make sure that, you know, gets integrated and the pipelines keep getting better and the product keeps getting better.
So that's kind of what I do here.
Pleased to meet you.
Happy to hear a little bit about you and then we can talk through a few topics.
Yeah, sure.
So I'm a B2B marketer.
That's how I describe my profession.
I have a background that is heavy in editorial work.
B2B I'm
Content production, end-to-end, and workflow optimization for the last one year or so.
I started as a writer, and then freelance writer, just churning out tons of articles, probably hundreds of articles I've written on the internet.
And then I worked as an editor for, I've worked in three marketing agencies, an email marketing agency where I was also managing their content, the agency's content.
And then I worked in an editorial role for Word 2.0, which is a white-label marketing agency as an editor.
And then I worked as managing editor for Reel & Limited before my role as content producer at remote.
So, yeah, that's generally the roles I've handled.
With regards to work, I was heavily just editing tons of work as an editor.
I would be under the briefs and the articles, edit, provide feedback to writers, and submit to clients.
And I was not managing the pipeline, content pipeline in that role.
was purely editorial.
At around that time, ChatGPT was also launching, and I had better access.
We had an AI content service in Web 2.0.
And then I went into my managing editor role, where I was also in charge of briefing the clients, onboarding them, and managing the workflow from when they submit the content.
Brief, I engaged with them on the strategy to approach the content.
And then once we are aligned, I assigned it to a writer.
Our writers were distributed in...
Different countries.
And then we deliver in 24, 48 hours.
So we had a product that was marketed as delivering in 24 hours.
So clients would pay for the – so we had several – so we had like a pay-as-you-go.
Once – one article and you go, then you can subscribe to 500 words a day.
So if you are on 500 words a day and you order a 2,000-word article and you get it in four days.
So basically that's how it works.
If they were doing things like – that speed says to me a lot of the content that was being produced was used for like paid campaigns, right?
Or like newsletters, do you think?
Because it doesn't seem like organic marketing would need that speed, you know?
It's needed because some of the work was for agencies.
Again, some of the work was white-labor.
Oh, I see.
So they were like subcontracting, basically.
Yeah.
Some, Yeah, okay.
So we did have direct –
And then we were subcontracting.
And then so we would even get like a hundred briefs for different blogs, blog titles and briefs.
So we'll just deliver slowly by slowly.
And then we had long form content, like for medical websites and stuff like that.
We had, you know, this was an agency that takes in everything, even entire website content creation.
create the homepage content, yeah, service descriptions, everything.
Product, everything.
So we would take in anything because I think at that time, when I joined, they just raised some funding.
So they were trying to find their space in the market.
And there was also AI company, yeah.
So yeah, it was a very enjoyable role.
And it exposed me to different types of bodies of work as far as web content is concerned.
And it also exposed me to what.
Talking with different clients, you know, if I get feedback from a client, I knew how to handle it.
And as managing editor, also exposed me to creating checklists so that our writers had the ability to create almost 100% accurate work because of how fast it was.
And then it also exposed me to just strategizing and publishing content on some of the CMSs for some clients.
It was a service we offered to some clients.
In my role as remote, it was narrowed down and a bit technical when I joined.
So I was publishing the content on the website, updating content, and then I also owned production of industry reports and some of the ungetted content, mostly getted and ungetted content.
So the goal, the team targets that we had was MQ Health.
And so I was part of the organic content production team.
Up until October last year, when there was a restructuring that happened, and I was just moved to a new role in marketing projects producer, and it involves production of campaigns end to end.
So what we have is actually similar to what I've seen, is what GrowthX pipeline looks like.

We get ideas from any member of the team, from customer success, from technical teams, engineering teams, anyone who has an idea, submits it through a form, it goes into a Notion database, a backlog in a Notion database, move it to production, work with life cycle marketing and performance marketing to create strategy, and then produce the content using ChatGPT.
So it has exposed me to also prompting prompt work, and then my responsibility here is also optimizing the workflow because it was a new project.
It was not familiar.
Okay, it was kind of familiar territory because I did that as managing editor, however it was.
When I was moved, I didn't see the potential until I was in it, and I really enjoyed it.
I've enjoyed the exposure to AI as well.
And so I've also been, it has exposed me to also AI agents because I think our entire process can be built into an agent.
But we are not yet there, but what we, the things we currently automate is content production.
And then we have, we edit, we have human editing and human QA through different stages of the pipeline, yeah.
Okay, this is great.
I mean, this touches on a lot of the things that I want to talk to you about.
So I kind of want to talk to a couple of things in more specific terms that will help me kind of understand where your head's at on the content side and then on the tooling side.
Let's start at the top.
So, like, when you're thinking about, like, how do you personally judge whether a piece of...
Content is high quality.
You know, how do you know it's good enough?
And I think that the follow-up question I have for that is like, how do you calibrate to client work?
Because obviously nothing is ever perfect.
And so I would also love to hear about that calibration to client work.
So how do you judge whether content is high enough quality and how do you know when it's good enough to ship?
So I always judge using the business goal that we are, the content is, we are creating the content to, you know, meet that measurable goal.
So I want the content to be clear and engaging, but also to have measurable outcomes.
So the content should be, should resonate with the audience.

It should have, you know, it should be factual and it should deliver the, you know, the idea in a very systematic flow, a natural flow, but at the same time it should be optimized to meet a measurable business.
So I look at both.
I do not just judge the keywords and the CTAs and all that.
I also look at whether the work itself is actually engaging to the readers.
And I know, of course, a lot of people create content to rank, which is important.
You know, when I was a managing editor, I always, if we were creating SEO content, I always provided SEO score to the client along with the article.
So it's important to create content that ranks, but also content that resonates.
So I have a checklist of like SEO and then editing and writing.
So when it comes to SEO, I look at whether there's an anchor text that matches the link that we are linking.
I look at whether we have primary keywords properly placed, secondary keywords.
I look at the SAP analysis and whether we are matching what we got from the SAPs.
And then I also go into looking at the content introduction.
The introduction captures the audience's attention.
And then a conclusion that ties properly.
I also look at the transitions are properly placed, the H1s and the meta title.
I look at the sub-headings and I also look at alignment to brand.
So I create a checklist with all these details and the brand guidelines and brand style, tone, as an additional layer of checklist so that we do not miss anything.
Additionally, nowadays, because of AI, I have to check for AI so that it's not just, there's no factual basis.
So we do not have AI, yeah.
let's talk about, drive a little bit on that.
Like, so if you, could you describe a time when you, like, had to jump in and use, like, AI tooling to improve a content process or a workflow?
Like, what was the problem that you were trying to solve or the problem that you saw?
And then what you did there to improve that?
Because you're right, like, you do have, human intervention is very important with LLMs, but we do work a lot with them.
And so I would love to hear, like, a practical example of the time that you did that.

So in my current role, when we started, because we were just getting ideas from the team, so the ideas may be just random thoughts that someone, you know, a team member had and thought this is an idea that we can turn into a campaign.
But I'd give you maybe an idea that it's more, you know, was more actionable.
Because when...
We get these ideas, we have our CMO and senior directors check them and rank them, so that if they align with what our quarterly goals are, that's when I move them to production and start the work.
But, so what happens with AI is when initially when we generated the content, sorry, oh, I thought you were frozen.
So when we generated the content.
Oh, I just sit very still sometimes.
Sometimes people are like, hey, wait, are you gone?
I'm like, no, I'm Sorry, go ahead.
Yeah, so the content was not very factual.
And actually, that's when I started to learn about prompt, you know, prompt engineering and just prompting in general.
So the content, the output of the content we would get initially was not very accurate.
It would just have random information from the internet that is not necessarily what was on our product, what our products were marketing.
Yeah, because when...
general information, but...
But not really like specific information that adheres to like their feature set or their product.
Yes.
Yeah.
So because some of these campaigns, we aligned them with product launches.
So this was a campaign that was aligning to a product launch.
So what I did and what I worked with the team to do at that point was to build prompt libraries.
So we went back to the prompts and I've built like a system where I start with the role architecture.
So I prompt like imagine you are, you know, you're a content producer or you are a payroll manager and you have a H-1B visa ban in the United States.
We want to market to this type of employers.
So I start with the role architecture and then I go into context.
In the context, I'd like to provide now our product information, data from previous campaigns.
I provide comparison.

Comparisons to other, you know, comparisons between our products and what H1 visa would offer, you know, and then I, so I add all this information, I add the value propositioning that we want to sell, and then, so I've, every time we have a challenge with the content, and I always look back to what was the prompt, and what can we fix in the prompt, so that we train the engine to give us more accurate information.

Really quickly, on like a technical detail there, are you, do you work within like a custom GPT, do you work in just a single chat per article, like how do you structure that, or if you use Claude or Gems or whatever you, else you use, just curious, like how do you structure that, when you're, when you're, working with a client that you know you're going to be doing a repetitive work for?
So, because remote is just an internal team, so we, we have enterprise GPT.
I've created like a prompt library where so I can save my prompts and I can always refer to, you know, train the model to refer to that.
But I also like to save my prompts manually in Notion.
Yeah, everybody should have their own little like prompt library, just like scratch pad, because you're always refining them, right?
Yeah, something else that personally I use to improve prompts is Prompt Genie.
It's an AI tool.
don't know if you've, yeah.
Yeah, there's a handful of them out there.
Prompt Cowboy, Prompt Genie, and a couple of others.
Yeah, so I use those too also.
Yeah.
know, yeah.
And then talk to me about like, this is a similar vein, but like an editorial process that you kind of came into.
You mentioned the one.

One just now, but I would love to hear about any other examples of, it doesn't have to be AI or LLM related, but it's kind of like an editorial process that you saw that was inefficient or unclear or whatever, and how you implemented some improvements there or saw an opportunity and kind of were able to affect some improvements.
Yeah, so before AI, because when I was working in the other agencies, there was no AI at that time.
We were using Grammarly, SEMrush to check RFs.
So before, at that time, as an editor, I used to create my own checklists.
And then I would, but when I started as managing editor, we did not really have a clear process of how to drive the content production pipeline.
So I created a content production pipeline from scratch, also tailored to the fact that we were delivering content in specific.
And the writers were based in different regions.
So I created a pipeline that starts with, so once production does not start until we are in alignment with the client, the client has accepted that, you know, this is the number of words.
The client has provided all the information we need if there was background information needed.
Then I go into building the strategy myself.
So as a managing editor, was involved in the beginning and the end in the creation of the strategy, the content strategy, and in the final QA.
So from strategy, so in the strategy, I will share with the writer that we are aiming for this SEO score.
We would like the keywords to be placed this way.
Please include the following headings.
And then keep it, this is the tone, this is the style.
I would share the privilege.
This work that was similar, I would also share the website of the client, if there was one, and then I would provide a timeline as to when they should deliver.
So please deliver this by a certain date, a certain time.
So I created that script, and then, so I'll just paste it for every assignment.
Once we have the, once we have the, the brief from the client approved, I would have the, I would copy that and just assign to a writer.

Then the, automatically from the writer, it would be assigned, once the writer completed it, it would be automatically assigned to, to an editor, and then from an editor to me, and then from me to the client, and if there was feedback, we'd get it.
Now the challenge there was, we do not have a project management system like Asana, which I've also used before to automate processes.
Yeah, so, yeah, we were using, I think the software is called ManyRequests, but you can customize it to your agency.
So it's a very limited application, I'd say.
We use linear internally a lot.
So, you know, it's similar to Asana, but a little bit more tasks-oriented, you know, less process.
But we also have, we use Airtable as well, and there are plenty of automations to be built there.
But, yeah, I get the pain when you don't have a system like that to build automations and flows.
You're like, oh, you know, how do I, how do I paste this stuff manually?
Yeah.
Yeah.
Cool.
That was more manual, but because now the writers were fully aware of how the entire process works.
So if they were assigned a piece of work, they would just know that they can communicate if they're not able to deliver.
Right.
that timeline.
And I'm able to do
And I was also assigning within a specific window.
So I did train the team a lot on the new pipeline because it was manual.
However, at Stremote, we used Asana previously.
Then we recently migrated to Monday.
And I was actually, I facilitated that process because I was in this marketing project's team role.
So, yeah, I did support the migration process from Asana to Monday.
Okay.
Got it.
Yeah, great.
Thank you.
I appreciate that.
And I'm happy.
We have a few minutes.
Are there any questions you have for me about the role or the way we work?
Anything like that?
Yeah.
So I've, of course, gone through the production process from the website.
But I just wanted to know how does some things, I have some questions.
How do the editors and the AI engineers, AI team collaborate?
That's my first question.
I'll leave you.
I'll Thank
Yeah, it's pretty straightforward.
So one, most of our Slack channels across the company are open.
So you will be able to see as like an editor working with the tooling, all of the stuff that the EPD team or engineering team is working on.
And so you can kind of see, oh, cool, they're like working on this feature, they're building this out.
Of course, we have linear as well.
So you can look at a project to see like, oh, has a ticket already been filed on this issue or that sort of thing.
So visibility is the number one thing.
So you do have some visibility into what's going on.
We are building our own tool.
It's not happening in this black box over here.
And then you just have like a text box and you're like, I hope this works, right?
Like you could kind of see what's going on, which is great.
Then the second way is direct interaction.
So you can like bring it up.
Like we have channels that are shared where it's like, hey, I can tag a client engineer, client ops engineer into this, or we have a rotation where the engineer.
And yours will be like, cool, I'm on client ops this week.

And so they'll, any issues, let's say as an example, like this week we had a pipeline that was like, you know, hey, I was wondering if the pipeline could take these specific sources of information, you know, versus general sources.
We want to make sure that the drafting step, the article drafting step incorporated this specific research.
And they're able to just tag an engineer and say, hey, can the pipeline do this?
And like, actually, yeah, it can.
It's already built in here.
Here's how you do it.
Otherwise, they'd be like, actually, no, we can't do that currently.
File a ticket.
And so we go, open up linear, file a ticket.
You, as an editor working with that process and trying to improve the output of the workflow over time, you have all of the context the engineers need.
Because you know what it's doing, what it's not doing, you know, what you need it to do.
And the simplest terms for this is, okay, I know what it takes for it to exit the pipeline, exit the tool, and then deliver to client.
I know what the difference is, the delta delta between those.
And the goal every day, your goal, in addition to delivering the work, is to just shrink that gap.
Basically, every day, just make your job easier.
Like, how can I make my life easier?
How can I make my job easier?

And you have the ability to directly influence the product and its improvements by articulating that clearly, know, documenting it, articulating it, either filing a ticket, making, you know, asking the general team, hey, is anybody else seeing this?
See if it's, like, a recurring issue.
Because the fact is, is that some of the pipelines right now are delivering work that's within a few minutes of delivery to the client.
That's how good some of the pipelines are working.
But that's not universal, because every client has really specific needs, and some of them, frankly, are pickier than others, you know?

And so you have to kind of see, hey, what's the difference between what I'm getting out of the pipeline and what I feel comfortable, according to your editorial judgment, which is why I asked you so many questions about it, like what I feel comfortable to look
We can send the client and hey, this is ready for your approval and perhaps publishing.
And that ability to say, hey, here are the things that I'm seeing.
Here are ways that I think they could be improved.
And here's like maybe even some prompts or some back and forth to show you what post-processing we're currently doing.
And then I would love for this to happen in the pipeline so that I did not have to do it out of the pipeline at a tool.
And that opportunity is available to you.
So those are the two major ways.
One, visibility.
You can see what's happening with the product.
And then the second way is articulation, filing tickets, or interacting directly with the engineers to say, hey, I've got this issue.
Do you think it's – is it me?
Is it the LLM?
Or is it the pipeline?
You know, where's the input here that needs to be changed?
Yeah.
I think I'll start at the end of that.
Yeah, it's nice not to just be like working on some product that gets delivered and you have no, you know, no interaction.
You're like, well, I wish I did this.
But it won't, and I have no way to say it should.
You know, it's different.
Yeah.
I think that's also something I appreciated about my content producer role in remote when I started.
Because I would suggest to them, we were using Contentful, CMS, in the beginning, but now it's HubSpot.
So, for instance, I'd suggest, can we do bulk updates?
And also, something we did was building page types, which helped us to upload a ton of content and speed up, you know, scale fast.
Something else, what's one thing you're excited about in the next phase of GrowthX?
Honestly, like, offering some of our tooling to the public.
So, right now, we're using all of our tooling internally, and all of our managing editors and directors and myself and everybody else who uses this tooling.
Because we use it.
Like, we don't just use it for clients, which, you know, we obviously, we use it to deliver work to clients.
But we also run...
Run our own publication on it.
We have a publication called AI-led growth.
We have our engineering blog, which is highly technical articles that are about, you know, LLM engineering.
And those articles are written using our tools.
So, like, we are dogfooding it quite a bit, they say.
You know, that's the term.
And we're using it a lot.

But it's getting very, very close to being able to offer it externally, at least some elements of it externally, because we know that the public would absolutely be able to use some of these tools out of the box, so to speak, because we've gotten them to a place where they're good enough.
There's some other parts of it that are just more expert driven.
So you could hand it to the public or to a general user, but it would be like, you know, driving a Ferrari, you know, when you have very little experience.
And so you're just, you know, hey, this is too squirrely for me.
I can't really get, you know, and that's why we have experts and humans in the loop and people who have good editorial minds.
But then, as you can see from the job description and the.

Questions I've asked you, also coupling that with the process and LLM tooling, because you know, it's like, yes, you have a hip pocket understanding of what good content is, but you also need to be able to manipulate the context and instructions that you give LLMs in order to get that good output.

So for the exciting thing for me is like, yes, keep refining the process internally, making the tool better, but then being able to offer some aspects of it that we've refined enough to people who are just, let's call it not casual, and it's not like they're not intelligent users, but they're just inexperienced with this kind of work and say, hey, put in the work to make this thing work for you, you know, so that's fun.
That'd be cool.
You know, one thing I'm actually, I'm excited about growthx is the fact that I actually know that this is an opportunity, a gap that exists in the market.
This is, this is a team, this team is filling a gap, because

I've actually interacted with a lot of growthx posts on LinkedIn, so I've been seeing, I'm aware it's something that is filling a gap that is much needed, and there are so many people who don't want to go to NHN or Zapier and start building.
Yeah, it's a high level of difficulty.
I mean, you work with it, you know.

It could be squirrely and hard to pin down, but people are trying to get deliverables out the door, and they just are like, oh, wait, now I have to I go, like, become a workflow expert, you know, just to, like, improve my productivity, and it's like, no, you don't.
You know, we're here for that, so you're right, and there's a gap there.

Yeah, also, from working even internally, you know, there's a lot of pressure on top leaders to implement AI in their teams and to see how this AI can be integrated and help them, you know, scale and all this, but not everyone knows how to go about it, so it's something that's much needed.
Okay, one last question, because we're out of time.
And I don't, because this is very specific to the road, how is success measured and how much editorial freedom do editors get in their work?
So how will success be measured?
Yeah.
Okay.
So two-parter.
So one, I'll take the editorial freedom part first.
Obviously, as you know, and as you told me, a lot of it has to do with client goals, like client outcomes and client goals.
Is this driving towards that?
The freedom in my mind, like a lot of it comes from the fact that we view that the managing editors as like a vital part of the process.
It's not just, hey, edit this brief and, you know, to this spec and that's it.
There is a loop, a feedback loop there that needs to exist between you understanding what the client wants and which is the transparency.
Like you're in the channel, you see everything the client's saying, you can see the calls that the account manager may be having with the client.
And, you know, their concerns or whatever.
And, of course, the.
Reporting dashboards.
You can see them.
You can see the accountability.
And you're driving towards an outcome.
We have talked to them about outcomes.
Okay, what do you want?
What do you need?
What can GrowthX help you with?
Maybe that's conversions.
Maybe it is organic traffic.
Maybe it's LLM visibility.
Maybe it's all of them.
Whatever.
Whatever.
Like, is this content going to drive towards that goal?
Is the question that you can ask yourself every day.
And you can have to say it, right?
Where it's like, hey, we can produce these all day long, but I have some real doubt that they will produce this outcome.
Either here's some data or some gut feeling or whatever.
And like that kind of interaction that happens at the managing editor level between them and like the account strategist or director on the account.
Like those things, that's a vital part of the whole process.
So it's not just, hey, here's some Google Docs, go edit these, right?
Like that's not what the role is at all.
So that's one.
And then the second part, which is tied to it, is the success metrics on the end.
Like a very pragmatic level, it's, hey, are you producing your work efficiently?
Are you getting more efficient over time?
Like, are you increasing your leverage?
So you maybe we start you on one client and you're producing the work for one client.
And maybe that's, I'll give you an example.
Maybe it's like five editorial articles, right?
And it starts out and you're like, wow, it took me about a week to get these five editorial articles in shape.
I have some suggestions on how we can improve the pipeline so that I can shrink that time.
These days, as an example, I run a small pod because I take on some clients coming out of our sprint process that are maybe a little tougher, a little thornier.
And then I, you know, kind of get that pipelines in shape.
Right.

And so like one of our clients came out where we were like, wow, I don't, you know, they, they have like a medical doctor review, uh, the content after we give it to them, a naturopathic doctor, a lawyer, a co-founder.
So, and like, and an OBGYN.
So like they're, they.
Really care, you know, about this, the quality.
And we were able to basically get it from a place where it was taking us a full week to get like five articles over to them for review that we thought would stand up to that kind of scrutiny.
And now we are able to produce those articles like on a Monday morning and run our editorial passes and deliver them by like Monday afternoon, Tuesday, you know.
And that leverage allows us the opportunity to go like, cool, give us another client, right?
Like now I have Wednesday through Friday, you know what mean?
Like now I have room for more.

So I think the measurement of success is obviously like getting the client work out on time, organizing yourself very, very crisply to get that, like having a good organizational, internal organizational structure in your own brain and your process so that you don't feel like you're behind the ball.
Because you should not feel like you're constantly behind the undeliverables and you should not feel like you have.
If work overtime to get deliverables done or work outside of work hours, if either of those things are happening, there is some failure in usually three major areas.
One, just general organization, like get organized, get the information you need, get the flow.
Sometimes that's client related.
Sometimes it's the client that's disorganized and that's okay.
Yeah.
Talk about that.
But the second is inputs.
Like what artifacts are you giving the pipeline?
Are they crisply defined?
The context, you know, what context are you giving the pipeline?
Is it crisply defined?
What instructions are you giving the pipeline?
Are those accurate?
And then the third one, which cannot be overlooked, we have to be honest, is the tooling.
Like is the pipeline just bad at its job?
And if so, how and why, right?
Like talk to me, chop, let's chop it up and figure out why.
Improving those three things, generally speaking, reduces that delta and improves your leverage and makes it so that you feel in command.
And you you can.
Get like one or two or three weeks ahead of client delivery and then have time to experiment, to suggest new work streams, to say, hey, you know what?
They've been talking about X.
I think we could spin up a whole new cluster or a whole new – you know what they really need?
A glossary section.
They don't have one.
Nobody else in this industry really has one.
We could do that for them.
And then we go, hey, you know what we could do?
We could charge you more and we could do a glossary for you.
You know what mean?
So that opportunity for expansion is always there.

But it's really hard to do that or think about that if you don't have leverage, if you feel like you're behind the ball on the current deliverables and you're always kind of like struggling to get it out.
So that process improvement is why we talked so much about that today.
It's just so important to this role because it really does require somebody who's like methodical, organized, says, hey, you know, I see ways to improve this process.
And then now, of course, let me work with engineering or work with other people on the delivery team to help get that.
Leverage, get that time back and be able to produce better.
Yeah.
love that.
Thank you for sharing in detail.
Of course.
Yeah.
Those are all the questions I have.
If you have anything you'd like me to expand more on, is there anything?

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Screening Hilda Kibet for the Managing Editor role at GrowthX.](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=363.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Relevant Experience:** Kibet's background as a Managing Editor (Reel & Limited) and Marketing Projects Producer (Remote) directly aligns with the role's need for end-to-end content production and workflow optimization.](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=407.0)
  - [**AI Expertise:** Kibet solved AI-generated factual errors by developing a prompt engineering system (Role → Context → Instruction) and using tools like Prompt Genie to improve output accuracy.](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=1023.0)
  - [**Process Improvement:** Kibet built a content pipeline from scratch at Reel & Limited and facilitated a project management tool migration (Asana → Monday) at Remote, demonstrating a track record of implementing efficient workflows.](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=1310.0)
  - [**Role Alignment:** The Managing Editor role requires both editorial judgment and a proactive, iterative approach to improving GrowthX's internal AI tools, with success measured by increasing efficiency and leverage.](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=2097.0)

## Topics

### Candidate Background & Experience

  - [**Reel & Limited (Managing Editor):**](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=461.0)
      - [Built a content production pipeline from scratch to meet 24–48 hour delivery SLAs for white-label agencies and direct clients.](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=1362.0)
      - [Managed the full content lifecycle: client strategy, writer assignments, and final QA.](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=1362.0)
      - [Created detailed checklists to ensure high-quality output from a distributed team.](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=667.0)
  - [**Remote (Marketing Projects Producer):**](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=690.0)
      - [Managed end-to-end campaign production, including gated/ungated content and industry reports.](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=690.0)
      - [Facilitated the company's project management tool migration from Asana to Monday.](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=1568.0)
      - [Developed a new workflow for campaigns, from idea submission (Notion) to production using enterprise GPT.](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=736.0)

### Content Quality & AI Workflow

  - [**Quality Judgment:**](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=834.0)
      - [Content must meet a measurable business goal (e.g., conversions, traffic).](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=834.0)
      - [Requires a balance of factual accuracy, audience engagement, and technical SEO optimization.](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=861.0)
      - [Uses a checklist covering SEO (SERP analysis, keywords), writing (flow, transitions), and brand alignment.](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=939.0)
  - [**AI Workflow Optimization:**](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=1023.0)
      - [**Problem:** Initial AI outputs were factually inaccurate and lacked product-specific details.](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=1092.0)
      - [**Solution:** Developed a prompt engineering system to train enterprise GPT for accuracy.](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=1109.0)
          - [**1. Role Architecture:** Define the AI's persona.](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=1153.0)
          - [**2. Context:** Provide specific product info, campaign data, and value propositions.](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=1153.0)
          - [**3. Instruction:** Give clear, detailed tasks.](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=1153.0)
      - [**Tools:** Uses enterprise GPT, Notion for prompt libraries, and Prompt Genie for refinement.](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=1233.0)

### GrowthX Role & Collaboration

  - [**Collaboration with Engineering:**](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=1595.0)
      - [**Visibility:** Open Slack channels and Linear provide transparency into EPD team work.](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=1611.0)
      - [**Direct Interaction:** Editors tag engineers in shared channels or file tickets to request pipeline improvements.](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=1667.0)
  - [**Role Expectations & Success Metrics:**](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=2097.0)
      - [**Editorial Freedom:** Editors are key strategic partners, not just executors. They drive outcomes and can challenge content that won't meet client goals.](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=2116.0)
      - [**Success Metrics:** Measured by increasing efficiency and leverage over time.](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=2215.0)
          - [**Example:** Reducing the time to produce 5 articles for a high-scrutiny client from one week to one day.](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=2237.0)
      - [**Leverage:** Frees up time for proactive work, like suggesting new content streams (e.g., a glossary) and driving account expansion.](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=2305.0)
      - [**Efficiency Drivers:** Success depends on improving three areas:](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=2340.0)
          - [**1. Organization:** Internal process management.](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=2340.0)
          - [**2. Inputs:** Providing crisp, defined context and instructions to the pipeline.](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=2340.0)
          - [**3. Tooling:** Identifying and fixing pipeline inefficiencies.](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=2340.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**Marilia:** Contact Hilda Kibet with next steps, including a potential project to assess process.](https://fathom.video/share/_LYd9e7kzzk5-fMUzawLFt-ELMhDDdtJ?tab=summary&timestamp=2476.0)

action_items.description: Email Hilda Kibet re: next steps + project details
